input.headline: George Washington faces Georgetown on 3-game losing streak

raw.headline: George Washington faces Georgetown on 3-game losing streak

winner: George Washington crosses the Delaware, loses three games of charades to a bunch of Hoyas.

candidates.text: Why did George Washington decide to play against Georgetown when on a three-game losing streak? Because he wanted to cross that bridge once more!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'cross that bridge' reference George Washington's famous crossing of the Delaware, adding historical specificity and surprise."}

candidates.text: George Washington was so disappointed with his recent losses that he decided to enroll at Georgetown University... and now he's on a 300-year-long losing streak!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Creative twist on historical figures, good use of specificity and surprise with the 300-year losing streak"}

candidates.text: Which founding father said, "Three strikes and you're Washington!"?
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'Three strikes and you're out' with a historical twist, concise and specific to the headline."}

candidates.text: George Washington crosses the Delaware, loses three games of charades to a bunch of Hoyas.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'crosses the Delaware' with 'loses three games of charades,' ties in Georgetown's mascot (Hoyas) for specificity and surprise."}

candidates.text: George Washington's struggling team is set to meet Georgetown – the founding father would probably prefer a signed treaty over this losing streak.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'founding father' and ties historical figure to sports context with clever twist."}
